FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), 21 research sites, 5.0 data release: "a large diverse nationwide sample of 9-10-year-olds at baseline (2016-2018) from 21 research sites across the U.S."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression and GSEM mediation on ABCD Study data (Years 1-3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Substance Use & Misuse, Vol. 59, No. 6, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: "social media time, hours (Year 1)" as IV yields RR=1.31 (95% CI 1.20-1.43, p<0.001) for alcohol sipping (Year 3), a substance-use individual-trait outcome (Table 2, Model 1). Phone/SM-specific variable directly paired with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric estimates reported: RR=1.31 for social media time on sipping; RR=1.03 for SMAQ on sipping; indirect effect B=0.011 (BC 95% CI 0.007, 0.016, p<0.001); proportion mediated=25.0% (Tables 2 and 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis conducted with numeric results including risk ratios, regression coefficients, and mediation path estimates throughout Tables 2 and 3

FT_EC7_reason: Social media time is the IV (predictor of alcohol sipping), not the DV; condition (b) of FT-EC7 not satisfied
notes: Social media time (IV) -> alcohol sipping (DV, substance use outcome); SMAQ also appears as mediator variable but social media time as IV is the qualifying analysis

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 4.0 release, diverse sample of 4,718 U.S. adolescents aged 10-15 from 21 sites: "This study analyzed data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multivariable linear and logistic regression on ABCD Study data (Years 2-3)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in BMC Public Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in main tables or supplements; all results use total recreational screen time only. Step 2: The composite screen time measure includes "television shows, movies, videos, video chat, single and multi-player video games, social media, and texting" (Methods, p.3) - this bundles TV shows, movies, and video games (non-phone/non-SM ingredients) with social media and texting, without a per-modality breakout in the results. No separate social media or texting coefficient appears in Table 2 or supplementary appendices. Composite fails Step 2 because it includes non-phone/non-SM items without separate breakout.

notes: Screen time composite explicitly includes TV shows/movies and video games alongside social media and texting, with no per-modality breakout in results; fails IC2 Step 2

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 4.0 release, 9,538 adolescents ages 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018, 21 recruitment sites: "longitudinal data from baseline to Year 2 from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study (4.0 release)"

FT_IC5.evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using mixed-effects models on ABCD Study data (baseline to Year 2 follow-up)

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in BMC Public Health, 2024

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 (Model 2, Adjusted) reports separate coefficients for social media (B=0.13, 95% CI 0.01, 0.26, p=0.036), video chat (B=0.35, 95% CI 0.19, 0.51, p<0.001), and texting (B=0.29, 95% CI 0.07, 0.51, p=0.012) as IVs predicting depressive symptoms (CBCL DSM-oriented scale, a mental-health individual-trait outcome). Phone/SM-specific estimates paired with individual-trait outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric B coefficients with 95% CIs across all CBCL DSM-oriented scales (depressive, anxiety, somatic, ADHD, ODD, conduct) for total screen time and six screen type subtypes including social media, video chat, and texting (Tables 2 and 3)

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential mixed-effects regression analyses conducted with numeric results throughout Tables 2 and 3 for SM/phone-specific variables paired with mental health outcomes

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time (including social media, texting, video chat) is the IV predicting mental health outcomes; SM is not the DV in any analysis; FT-EC7 condition (b) not satisfied
notes: Paper reports individual coefficients for social media (B=0.13), video chat (B=0.35), and texting (B=0.29) as separate IVs predicting depressive symptoms in Table 2 Model 2 Adjusted, qualifying under IC2 Step 1
